reasoning: The matching for Q1 is based on the features in the structures. Structure (a) is the simple 1sg subject prefix, matching e-. Structure (b) is 3sg subject with 1sg object incorporated, matching aa-. Structure (c) is 3sg subject with 3sg object incorporated, matching a-. Structure (d) is the special no suffix structure, matching *none*. Structure (e) is the benefactive suffix structure, matching -oki. Structure (f) is the singular person noun structure, matching lmurani. Structure (g) is the plural person noun structure, matching lmuráni. The patterns in the diagrams show PERS and NUM features aligning with these forms, and the Maasai word parts fit the missing components in the labeled structures.

The invalid sentences are A and G. These are the sentences with two nouns (lmurani lecta, person and tree) but without the benefactive suffix -oki. The no-suffix structure has only one object position (OBJECT1 under), so two nouns cannot both unify with the verb structure—one noun would have no position to unify with. Sentences with -oki have two object positions (OBJECT1 ben, OBJECT2 under), allowing two nouns. All other sentences have either one noun or -oki with two nouns, allowing unification. The patterns show A and G as the only such cases without -oki and two nouns.

For -oki sentences with two nouns, lmurani = beneficiary (object1 ben), lecta = patient (object2 under). For -oki with one lmurani, lmurani = beneficiary, patient empty (pro 'it' from PERS:3 NUM:sg CASE:acc = him/her/it, but contextually 'it'). For -oki with one lecta, lecta = patient, beneficiary empty (pro 'him/her' from PERS:3 NUM:sg ANIM:animate CASE:acc). For no -oki with one noun, the noun = patient (object1 under).
